dropped.reason: too short to carry a claim

meta.model: Gemini gemini-flash-lite-latest · US (and trains on free-tier prompts)

clusters.label: Staff compensation and uncompetitive market salaries
Opaque leadership decisions and communication gaps
Unfulfilled promises regarding titles and promotions
Academic quality below expectations relative to costs

insights.title: The total absence of structured onboarding leaves new hires stranded without basic training or guidance.
insights.summary: New employees arrive to find no formalized onboarding process beyond receiving a laptop, forcing them to learn entirely through osmosis. This chaotic introduction contrasts sharply with the polished external image of the institution. Basic administrative processes and reception inquiries are handled poorly, creating unnecessary friction for both staff and visitors.

evidence.name: PA
evidence.quote: Complete lack of onboarding. First day? They give you a laptop and that's it. Who trains you? Nobody. You learn through osmosis or you don't. It's not professional for a company…

evidence.name: PA
evidence.quote: Many positive aspects. But the company is disorganized in some fundamental processes. Recruitment unclear, onboarding superficial, training absent. You'll improve once you fix…

evidence.name: PA
evidence.quote: Young environment but sometimes unprofessional. Inefficient meetings, chaotic decisions, absent documentation. Passion is high, but organization doesn't match the mission.

recommendation.cost: These are new hires questioning their decision on the very first day, when they find the organisation has none of the professional infrastructure its brand promises.
recommendation.action: Deploy a standardized two-week onboarding checklist and assign a dedicated mentor to every incoming employee.
recommendation.rationale: Relying on osmosis wastes weeks of productivity and signals to incoming talent that operational standards do not match the brand's outward ambition.
recommendation.signal: new hires complete structured training milestones within their first month and onboarding feedback scores improve consistently

insights.title: Uncompetitive compensation drives away experienced staff who feel exploited by mission-focused justifications.
insights.summary: Employees report that salaries fall drastically below market rates, leading to heavy turnover among senior talent. While staff appreciate the organizational mission, the low base pay and uncompetitive raises create resentment. Workers observe peers in similar roles earning significantly more elsewhere. Relying purely on enthusiasm to retain staff has reached its limit, resulting in the steady departure of capable professionals.

evidence.name: PA
evidence.quote: Salaries are an affront compared to the market. Friends in similar companies earn double. H-Farm hires young inexperienced people and pays them little until they leave. It's…

evidence.name: PA
evidence.quote: Salaries are genuinely penalizing. You could do proper market salary analysis and align. Instead, there's little interest in retaining talent. I have friends doing my job and…

evidence.name: PA
evidence.quote: Stimulating environment, but salaries are genuinely below market average. I made more money two years ago at a smaller company. If you're here, it's for the mission, not the money.

recommendation.cost: these are skilled employees realizing their pay is entirely disconnected from market value, and senior professionals are quietly walking away to competitors who offer realistic compensation
recommendation.action: Conduct a comprehensive market salary adjustment to align base pay with regional technology sector benchmarks.
recommendation.rationale: Bonuses and mission statements cannot substitute for competitive base pay when the gap reaches the magnitudes reported by staff. Small increments will fail to stop the exodus.
recommendation.signal: employee retention rates stabilize and exit interviews stop citing uncompetitive salaries as the primary reason for leaving

insights.title: Leadership opacity regarding strategic decisions leaves teams working in the dark and fosters institutional distrust.
insights.summary: Internal communication suffers from severe bottlenecks where strategy is withheld and project cancellations come as complete surprises. Employees describe learning about major changes through informal channels rather than direct leadership updates. This lack of transparency alienates staff who want to contribute effectively. Without regular and structured updates from management, employees feel disconnected from the institutional direction.

evidence.name: PA
evidence.quote: Communication is a nightmare. Leadership doesn't share strategies, doesn't explain decisions, doesn't listen to feedback. You feel like you're working in the dark. Sometimes you…

evidence.name: PA
evidence.quote: Good, but there are communication issues. Sometimes you don't understand senior decisions until it's too late to impact them. More transparency would help. Otherwise, it's a nice…

evidence.name: PA
evidence.quote: Good culture, but communication could improve. You often discover new projects through gossip, not official channels. Leadership should be more transparent about decisions.

recommendation.cost: these are dedicated team members losing trust in leadership competence at the exact moment they discover critical project changes through gossip instead of official channels
recommendation.action: Establish mandatory bi-weekly all-hands meetings where leadership explicitly details strategic changes, ongoing projects, and upcoming operational decisions.
recommendation.rationale: The issue is not a lack of collaboration tools but a failure of leadership to cascade information downward before decisions take effect in the field.
recommendation.signal: internal surveys show increased satisfaction with leadership communication and staff stop discovering project cancellations through gossip
